nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing tropical freshwaters — ponds, paddies, swamps, ditches, and quiet backwaters across the South and Southeast Asian lowlands.
nativeRange.biotope: South / Southeast Asian lowland still freshwater

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs near the bulb
open midground footprint with vertical headroom

avoidWith: fish that uproot bulbs (e.g. large barbs in groups)

summary: The Dwarf Aquarium Lily (Nymphaea stellata) is a compact bulb plant from South and Southeast Asia, prized for its reddish-bronze to pinkish-green arrow-shaped submerged leaves. It stays noticeably smaller than the more familiar Tiger Lotus, making it well suited to nano and small community aquariums.
careNotes: The Dwarf Aquarium Lily (Nymphaea stellata) is a true bulb plant of the family Nymphaeaceae, native to lowland still and slow-moving freshwaters across India and Southeast Asia. Like other members of the genus Nymphaea, it produces two distinct leaf forms: arrow-shaped to spade-shaped submerged leaves emerging directly from the bulb (typically reddish-bronze to pinkish-green under good light) and long-stemmed floating lily pads that, if allowed to reach the surface, will canopy the tank and shade plants below. To keep the plant in its compact, fully-submerged form, trim floating stems at the base before they reach the surface; allow a few stems through and it will create surface shade and may flower. Plant the bulb sitting on the substrate with the leaves and growing tip facing upward and the roots pointing down — bury only about the bottom half of the bulb, leaving the upper half exposed, as fully burying the bulb tends to cause it to rot. Newly placed bulbs may float briefly before water-logging and sinking. The species is generally considered hardy across 22–28 °C in soft to moderately hard water (pH 6.0–7.5), and tolerates low to high light, though brighter lighting and added iron tend to yield richer red colour and more compact growth. CO2 is not required but improves growth rate and colour. Compared to the more familiar Tiger Lotus (Nymphaea zenkeri), the Dwarf Aquarium Lily stays substantially smaller — roughly 10–30 cm in typical submerged form versus 20–60 cm for Tiger Lotus — making it a better fit for nano and small community aquariums.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily in the aquarium via daughter plants on side shoots that emerge from the parent bulb once it is well established; cut the side shoots free and replant. Mature plants whose floating leaves reach the surface of an open-top tank can flower (star-shaped white-to-pinkish blooms, the source of the name 'stellata') and occasionally set seed, but vegetative propagation from daughter bulbs and side shoots is the standard hobby method.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary care reference. Confirms scientific name Nymphaea stellata, origin India and Southeast Asia, temperature 72–82 °F (22–28 °C), low-to-high lighting tolerance, no CO2 requirement, fast growth once established, midground-to-background placement, planting with bulb partially exposed (do not fully bury), arrow-shaped reddish-bronze to pinkish-green submerged leaves alongside floating lily pads, propagation by cutting daughter-plant side shoots, and use of liquid fertilizers plus root tabs.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (71–83 °F ≈ 21.7–28.3 °C), Indian origin, moderate lighting with brighter light supporting compact growth and stronger red colour, optional CO2, moderate growth rate, midground placement, easy care level, ~5 in / ~13 cm typical size, spade-shaped leaves with brownish-pink to deeper red tones, planting with bulb only partially buried to reduce rot risk, and iron-rich fertilization for deeper reds.
